construction_type: Type IIA or IIIA depending height/area and jurisdiction

parti_config.stacking_logic: public low / secure-service internal / MEP stacked

plan_archetypes: public lobby with screened entry; separate public/judicial/in-custody circulation
courtroom bar with stacked court sets

sources.title: U.S. Courts Design Guide
sources.publisher: Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts

sources.title: Time-Saver Standards for Building Types — Courthouses

medium.name: Federal Courthouse (typical)
medium.notes: Multiple courtrooms; judicial chambers; secure circulation separation

large.name: Large Urban Justice Center
large.notes: Many courtrooms; holding; sally port; clerk and records

ibc_primary_occupancy: A-3 / B / I-3 accessory where holding is provided

space_library.name: Security Entry / Screening
notes: Queue area often drives size; directly precedes public lobby

space_library.name: Public Lobby / Waiting
notes: Wayfinding and queuing; often daylit grand hall

space_library.name: Courtroom
notes: Size varies by jurisdiction, jury box, and case type

space_library.name: Judge Chambers Suite
notes: Typically private office + secretary + robing/secure access

space_library.name: Records / Evidence / File Storage
notes: High-density storage may drive structure and fire protection

space_library.name: In-Custody Holding / Sally Port Support
notes: Used only where custody functions are part of the program

space_library.name: Screening Ops / Guard Post
notes: Visual control of entry and lobby

space_library.name: Public Vertical Circulation
notes: Elevators/stairs sized to separate public and secure flows where needed

space_library.name: Public Restrooms
notes: Public restrooms distributed near lobby, courtrooms, and jury assembly; sized per IPC fixture counts.

space_library.name: Public Circulation
notes: Primary public corridor system connecting lobby, courtrooms, and public support spaces; separated from secure and judicial circulation.

space_library.name: Secure Circulation
notes: Restricted corridor system for judicial staff and in-custody movement; fully separated from public circulation with controlled access points.
